interactions.summary: Jiang contrasts medieval Christian art with Renaissance art: stained glass is high, illuminated, blinding, and submissive, while Da Vinci's Last Supper uses depth to pull the viewer into the event.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang; seg-0018 is a single unclear 'Okay?' likely lecture filler rather than a separate question.

interactions.summary: He reads The Last Supper as a human drama of betrayal: the incomplete table expands toward the viewer, the apostles' bodies and hands disclose fear, and Judas is marked subtly by darkness, silver, and tension rather than by a crude halo contrast.

speaker_notes.note: The diarizer marks a one-word UNKNOWN span, but it functions as an 'Okay?' transition and not a substantive student question.

claims.claim: Medieval Christian art is presented as idea-centered and submissive: its stained glass overwhelms the viewer with divine awesomeness rather than inviting imaginative participation.
Renaissance art moves from 'blinding and awesome' to 'compelling and curious' by using depth, spatial motion, and dramatic tension to make the viewer participate.
claims.temporal_scope: Renaissance art model in this lecture.

claims.claim: Da Vinci's Last Supper forces investigation because Judas is not obviously isolated; the viewer must read faces, hands, light, coins, and bodily tension to discover betrayal.
claims.temporal_scope: Interpretation of The Last Supper in this lecture.

claims.claim: Jiang treats Da Vinci as an astute observer of emotion whose art turns anatomy, facial expression, breathing, eyes, and hand motion into evidence of inner life.
claims.temporal_scope: Interpretation of Da Vinci in this lecture.

signature_moments.moment: Christian stained glass is not there for you to enter; it is there to make you submit.
signature_moments.source_phrase: You're not here to participate. You're here to submit.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This sharp contrast carries the whole art-history transition from obedience to participation.

signature_moments.moment: The too-thin table in The Last Supper expands outward until the viewer becomes part of the picture.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It makes viewer participation concrete instead of merely aesthetic.

signature_moments.moment: Judas is betrayed not by a label but by darkness, silver, and a neck too tense to breathe.
signature_moments.source_phrase: all the tension is captured in his neck
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It shows how Renaissance art turns moral drama into embodied clues.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang's phrase for medieval Christian art that overwhelms the observer with divine mystery and discourages participation.
Jiang's phrase for Renaissance art that draws the viewer into the scene and makes interpretation necessary.

uncertainty_notes.note: Some Last Supper visual-detail wording is ASR-damaged, but the interpretive structure is clear from surrounding segments.
